Discusses mechanisms, functional implications for memory encoding and credit assignment, and how BTSP differs from classical STDP/LTP paradigms.  
![Figure preview](https://media.springernature.com/m685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41593-026-02214-2/MediaObjects/41593_2026_2214_Fig1_HTML.png)

### 2) Synaptic basis of feature selectivity in hippocampal neurons  
**Source:** Nature (2024-12-18)  
**Summary:** Presents an all-optical approach to monitor synaptic weight changes of individual dendritic spines in awake, behaving mice during place-field induction. Demonstrates temporally asymmetric, bidirectional synaptic weight changes associated with place-field formation and compartment-specific differences (basal vs oblique dendrites). Links synaptic plasticity to rapid emergence of spatial selectivity.  
![Figure preview](https://media.springernature.com/m685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41586-024-08325-9/MediaObjects/41586_2024_8325_Fig1_HTML.png)  
[View Details (Nature PDF)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-024-08325-9.pdf)

---

### 3) Synaptic plasticity rules driving representational shifting in the hippocampus  
**Source:** Nature Neuroscience (2025-03-20)  
**Summary:** Combines computational modeling and experimental place-field data to compare plasticity rules (BTSP vs STDP). Finds BTSP better explains trial-by-trial place-field shifting dynamics in CA1 and notes differences in BTSP frequency/phenomenology between CA1 and CA3. Provides a framework for continuous representational reshaping during learning.  
![Figure preview](https://media.springernature.com/m685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41593-025-01894-6/MediaObjects/41593_2025_1894_Fig1_HTML.png)  
[View Details (Nature Neuroscience)](http://www.npg.nature.com/articles/s41593-025-01894-6)

---

### 4) The Intriguing Contribution of Hippocampal Long-Term Depression to Spatial Learning and Long-Term Memory  
**Source:** Frontiers in Behavioral Neuroscience (2022-04-25)  
**Summary:** Review focusing on hippocampal long-term depression (LTD). Argues that LTD plays active roles in spatial learning, retention of content details, synaptic pruning, and pattern separation — not merely as a complement to LTP. Discusses structural spine changes and behavioral/electrophysiological evidence from rodents.  
![Figure preview](https://images-provider.frontiersin.org/api/ipx/w=1200&f=png/https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/806356/fnbeh-16-806356-HTML-r1/image_m/fnbeh-16-806356-g001.jpg)  
[View Details (Frontiers)](https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fnbeh.2022.806356/full)

---

### 5) Hippocampal OLM interneurons regulate CA1 place cell plasticity and remapping  
**Source:** Nature Communications (2025-11-11)  
**Summary:** Shows OLM interneurons (which target distal CA1 dendrites) control dendritic Ca2+ signaling and thereby gate associative synaptic plasticity at Schaffer collateral synapses. Reduced OLM activity in novel environments facilitates plasticity and place-cell remapping. Manipulating OLM activity alters place-cell stability and remapping dynamics.  
![Figure preview](https://media.springernature.com/m685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-025-64859-0/MediaObjects/41467_2025_64859_Fig1_HTML.png)  
[View Details (Nature Communications)](http://www.npg.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-64859-0)

---

### 6) Convergence and divergence of molecular mechanisms in Hebbian and homeostatic plasticity  
**Source:** Frontiers in Synaptic Neuroscience (2026-02-09)  
**Summary:** Review comparing molecular pathways underlying Hebbian forms (LTP/LTD) and homeostatic synaptic scaling. Highlights shared and distinct signaling mechanisms regulating AMPAR trafficking, scaffolding, cell-adhesion, and secreted factors — relevant to how hippocampal circuits balance synapse-specific learning with global stability.  
![Figure preview](https://images-provider.frontiersin.org/api/ipx/w=1200&f=png/https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1761008/fnsyn-18-1761008-HTML/image_m/fnsyn-18-1761008-g001.jpg)

## Overview

Below are examples showing how to retrieve (list) all CellMorphology entities using the Open Brain Platform Python SDK (entitysdk). I include:

- A minimal example that fetches a limited set (safe default).
- A full \u201cget all\u201d pattern that pages through results until all items are retrieved.
- Notes about pagination and total_items (required by EntityCore rules).

All examples assume you have an authenticated `client` instance from the entitysdk (see your project/docs for authentication). Replace any placeholder values (API keys, endpoints) per your environment.

---

## 1) Minimal / quick preview (limit results)

This fetches a limited number of morphologies (e.g., 10) \u2014 useful for quick checks.

```python
# Example: quick fetch (limit 10)
from entitysdk import Client
from entitysdk.entities import CellMorphology

# instantiate client (fill in auth details per your setup)
client = Client(api_key=\YOUR_API_KEY\")  # adapt to your auth method

# search for CellMorphology with a limit (safe preview)
morphs = client.search_entity(entity_type=CellMorphology, limit=10).all()

print(f\Retrieved {len(morphs)} morphologies (preview)\")
for m in morphs:
    print(m.id, m.name)
```

---

## 2) Recommended: Retrieve ALL morphologies with pagination (robust)

The EntityCore \u201cGET ALL\u201d may paginate. This pattern requests pages until all items are retrieved. It also prints the reported total_items so you can decide whether to continue if needed.

```python
from entitysdk import Client
from entitysdk.entities import CellMorphology

client = Client(api_key=\YOUR_API_KEY\")

# page_size should be <= service max (commonly 10); adapt if docs show different max
page_size = 10
page = 1
all_morphs = []

while True:
    result = client.search_entity(
        entity_type=CellMorphology,
        limit=page_size,
        offset=(page - 1) * page_size  # note: some clients use page/offset; adjust if client API differs
    ).page(page)  # if client provides a page method; otherwise use offset/limit pattern

    # If your client returns a wrapper with items and total_items:
    items = result.items if hasattr(result, \items\") else result  # adapt to the client response structure
    total_items = getattr(result,       otal_items\", None)

    # Collect items
    all_morphs.extend(items)

    # Display progress
    if total_items is not None:
        print(f\Fetched page {page}. collected {len(all_morphs)} of {total_items}\")

    # Stop when fewer items returned than page_size OR we've reached total_items
    if len(items) < page_size:
        break
    if total_items is not None and len(all_morphs) >= total_items:
        break

    page += 1

print(f\Total morphologies retrieved: {len(all_morphs)}\")
```

Notes:
- The entitysdk has multiple helpers; if it exposes a `.all()` or `.iterate()` convenience that automatically pages, prefer that. Example from docs: `client.search_entity(entity_type=CellMorphology).all()`.
- Always check the client response structure for fields like `items`, `total_items`, `limit`, `offset`, or built-in iterators.

---

## 3) Using the entitysdk convenience method shown in examples (single call)

The entitysdk examples show a convenient pattern using search_entity(...).all() to fetch results. If the SDK supports retrieving all with `.all()`, use that:


- The EntityCore examples show `client.search_entity(...).all()` as a convenient approach; it may already page for you.

morphology_studies.user: What are some recent studies on neuron morphology in the cerebrum?
morphology_studies.ai_response:
I'll search the literature for recent papers on neuron morphology in the cerebrum.## Recent studies on neuron morphology in the cerebrum — search summary

I searched recent literature for studies focused on neuronal morphology in the cerebrum / cerebral cortex. Below are key recent papers returned by the search, with short summaries using only the information retrieved from the literature tool and direct links to the articles.

### 1) **Pyramidal neurons proportionately alter cortical interneuron subtypes** — Nature (2026)
- **Citation / Link:** [Pyramidal neurons proportionately alter cortical interneuron subtypes | Nature](http://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-025-09996-8)  
- **Summary:** Shows that pyramidal neurons influence the survival and terminal differentiation of associated cortical interneuron subtypes. In Fezf2 mutants (loss of L5b pyramidal tract neurons and expansion of L6 intratelencephalic neurons), corresponding shifts occur in interneuron composition via subtype-specific mechanisms (e.g., SST interneurons via programmed cell death; PVALB interneurons via identity switching).  
- **Figure image:**  
  ![Fig 1](https://media.springernature.com/m685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41586-025-09996-8/MediaObjects/41586_2025_9996_Fig1_HTML.png)

---

### 2) **An unsupervised map of excitatory neuron dendritic morphology in the mouse visual cortex** — Nature Communications (preprint / article PDF)
- **Citation / Link:** [An unsupervised map of excitatory neuron dendritic morphology in the mouse visual cortex (PDF)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-58763-w.pdf)  
- **Summary:** Data-driven, graph-based machine learning analysis of >30,000 excitatory neuron reconstructions (mouse visual cortex MICrONS EM volume). Suggests excitatory neuron dendritic morphologies form a continuum (rather than discrete m‑types) with layer- and area-specific axes of variation; identifies notable exceptions in L5 and L6. Describes trends across layers and inter-area differences (e.g., layer 4 differences between V1 and higher visual areas).  
- **Note:** PDF available via the link above.

---

### 3) **Electrophysiological classification of human layer 2–3 pyramidal neurons reveals subtype-specific synaptic interactions** — Nature Neuroscience (2025 / 2026 volume)
- **Citation / Link:** [Electrophysiological classification of human L2–3 pyramidal neurons | Nature Neuroscience](http://www.nature.com/articles/s41593-025-02134-7)  
- **Summary:** Whole-cell recordings from >1,400 human L2–3 pyramidal neurons and ~1,400 identified monosynaptic connections in temporal cortex. Classifies pyramidal neurons into four electrophysiological subtypes that are corroborated by morphological differences and subtype-specific synaptic interactions; highlights fine microcircuit specialization in human superficial cortex.  
- **Figure image:**  
  ![Fig 1](https://media.springernature.com/m685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41593-025-02134-7/MediaObjects/41593_2025_2134_Fig1_HTML.png)  
- **PDF (full text) link:** https://nature.com/articles/s41593-025-02134-7.pdf

---

### 4) **Pyramidal neurons proportionately alter the identity and survival of specific cortical interneuron subtypes** — PubMed entry (links to Nature article)
- **Citation / Link:** [PubMed summary (Wu SJ et al., Nature, 2026)](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39149479/)  
- **Summary:** PubMed entry corresponding to the Nature 2026 paper above. Includes abstract and indexing details (DOI: 10.1038/s41586-025-09996-8). Useful if you prefer PubMed access or want metadata/PMID.

---

### 5) **Neuronal diversity and stereotypy at multiple scales through whole brain morphometry** — Nature Communications (2024)
- **Citation / Link:** [Neuronal diversity and stereotypy at multiple scales through whole brain morphometry (PDF)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-54745-6.pdf?error=cookies_not_supported&code=cc735ebf-3807-49b8-8b67-cf0b21d7f976)  
- **Summary:** Large-scale whole-brain morphometry across modalities (STPT, LSFM, fMOST) and registration to the Allen CCF. Annotated ~182,497 cell bodies, characterized ~15,441 dendritic microenvironments, reconstructed full morphology for 1,876 neurons, and detected ~2.63 million axonal varicosities. Provides multi-scale quantification of morphological diversity and stereotypy across the mouse brain—relevant for mapping cerebral neuronal morphologies at population scale.

---

I'll check the platform documentation for the definition of an ME-model.## Definition — **ME-model**

**ME-model** stands for **Morphology and Electrophysiology model**.  
It is a *multi-compartment electrical model* of a neuron that combines a reconstructed neuron morphology with its electrophysiological properties so the cell can be simulated as a spatially extended, biophysically-detailed electrical model.

### Key points
- **Full name:** *Morphology and Electrophysiology Model* (ME-model)  
- **Core components:**
  - Detailed neuron morphology (compartments: soma, dendrites, axon, etc.)
  - Electrophysiological properties (ion channel distributions, membrane parameters, synaptic inputs, stimulation protocols)
- **Purpose:** Simulate the electrical behavior of single neurons (voltage traces, spike times, current flow) using multi-compartment biophysical simulation engines.
- **Usage on the platform:** ME-models are used for single-cell simulations (including synaptome variants), configured via MEModelSimulation workflows and runnable through the platform’s single-cell simulation tools.

## Single Cell Simulation Example (OBI-One)

Below is a step-by-step single-cell simulation example using the OBI-One Python API (openbraininstitute/obi-one). It shows how to:

- select an MEModel (morphology+electrical model),
- build and validate a simulation configuration (stimulus, recordings, timestamps),
- generate a grid-scan / single simulation,
- and run the simulation with BlueCelluLab (the usual backend used in OBI-One examples).

You will need obi_one installed and configured, plus credentials / db_client as required by your environment. Replace placeholder IDs (e.g., <MEMODEL-ID>) and file paths with real values.

### 1) Select an MEModel to simulate
```python
# Option A: use a known MEModel ID
entity_ID = \"<MEMODEL-ID>\  # <<< Replace with real MEModel UUID

# Option B: (interactive selection helper used in examples)
# memodel_ids = get_entities.get_entities(\memodel\", token, memodel_ids,
#                                         project_context=project_context,
#                                         multi_select=False,
#                                         default_scale=\small\")
# memodel_ids[0] would then be used below
```

### 2) Build the MEModel simulation configuration (form API)
```python
from pathlib import Path
import obi_one as obi

# === Parameters ===
sim_duration = 3000.0  # ms

# Create an empty config for MEModel-based simulation
sim_conf = obi.MEModelSimulationScanConfig.empty_config()

# Info block
info = obi.Info(
    campaign_name=\MEModel Simulation\",
    campaign_description=\Single-cell MEModel simulation with constant current stimulus\"
)
sim_conf.set(info, name=\info\")

# Regular timestamps (example: a single repetition covering simulation)
regular_timestamps = obi.RegularTimestamps(start_time=0.0, number_of_repetitions=1, interval=sim_duration)
sim_conf.add(regular_timestamps, name='RegularTimestamps')

# Somatic current clamp stimulus (Constant current)
stimulus = obi.ConstantCurrentClampSomaticStimulus(
    timestamps=regular_timestamps.ref,
    duration=2000.0,
    amplitude=0.5  # nA (example)
)
sim_conf.add(stimulus, name=\CurrentClampInput\")

# Record soma voltage
voltage_recording = obi.SomaVoltageRecording()
sim_conf.add(voltage_recording, name='VoltageRecording')

# Optionally limit the time window recorded
time_window_voltage_recording = obi.TimeWindowSomaVoltageRecording(start_time=0.0, end_time=2000.0)
sim_conf.add(time_window_voltage_recording, name='TimeWindowVoltageRecording')

# Initialization: point to the MEModel by ID and set simulation length
simulations_initialize = obi.MEModelSimulationScanConfig.Initialize(
    circuit=obi.MEModelFromID(id_str=entity_ID),
    simulation_length=sim_duration
)
sim_conf.set(simulations_initialize, name='initialize')

# Validate and get validated configuration object
validated_sim_conf = sim_conf.validated_config()
print(\Validated config:\", validated_sim_conf)
```

### 3) Wrap config into a GridScan / single simulation configuration
(OBI-One uses GridScan tasks to generate concrete config files; for a single point you still create a grid with a single cell)
```python
# Create a GridScan generation task for the validated form
grid_scan = obi.GridScanGenerationTask(
    form=validated_sim_conf,
    coordinate_directory_option=\ZERO_INDEX\",
    output_root='../../../obi-output/memodel_simulations/grid_scan'  # change to desired output
)

# (Optional) inspect multi-value parameters or coordinates
grid_scan.multiple_value_parameters(display=True)
grid_scan.coordinate_parameters(display=True)

# Execute generation (requires db_client configured)
grid_scan.execute(db_client=db_client)
```

### 4) Run the generated simulation with BlueCelluLab backend
```python
from obi_one.scientific.library.simulation_execution import run

# Path to simulation_config.json from generated single config
simulation_config_path = grid_scan.single_configs[0].coordinate_output_root / \simulation_config.json\"
print(\Simulation config path:\", simulation_config_path)

# Run the simulation (bluecellulab is typical)
run(
    simulation_config=simulation_config_path,
    simulatorluecellulab\",  # or 
eurodamus\"
    save_nwb=False  # optionally save NWB
)
```

### 5) Load and analyze results with bluepysnap
```python
import bluepysnap

snap_simulation = bluepysnap.Simulation(simulation_config_path)
spikes = snap_simulation.spikes

print(\spikes time_start, time_stop, dt:\", spikes.time_start, spikes.time_stop, spikes.dt)
print(\population names:\", spikes.population_names)

# Access soma voltage report
soma_report = snap_simulation.reports['SomaVoltage']
print(\SomaVoltage time_start, time_stop, dt:\", soma_report.time_start, soma_report.time_stop, soma_report.dt)

# Example: inspect the head of the spike report dataframe
filtered = spikes.filter(t_start=spikes.time_start, t_stop=spikes.time_stop)
print(filtered.report.head())
```

## Notes, tips and placeholders
- **Replace** <MEMODEL-ID> with the actual MEModel UUID from EntityCore.
- You need a configured db_client variable (OBI-One examples use a DB client for GridScan generation) \u2014 ensure you have credentials and environment set up as per your installation.
- If you prefer to pick an MEModel interactively or via EntityCore queries, use the platform's EntityCore helpers to get MEModel IDs (examples in the repository show interactive selection).
- The examples above are adapted from the OBI-One example notebooks:
  - examples/F_single_cell_simulations/entitysdk_memodel_simulation.ipynb
  - examples/F_single_cell_simulations/entitysdk_memodel_with_synapses_simulation.ipynb

If you want, I can:
- provide a minimal runnable script adjusted to your environment (if you give me the MEModel ID and where your db_client/config is stored),
- or show how to query EntityCore for available MEModels and pick one programmatically. Which would you like?
